name: Vision
subtitle: Level 3 · Organised · Aligned Vision
whatThisIs:
Vision is where strategy begins. Not the corporate mission statement on your wall. Not the one-liner you trot out at networking events. The real picture of what you are building and why it matters. When vision is clear, every decision becomes easier because you know whether it moves you toward that future or away from it.

A clear vision prevents short-term optimisation from derailing long-term ambition. It provides coherence across decisions and ensures growth serves a defined future rather than random expansion. This playbook helps you define a specific, tangible 3-5 year picture of your business and align everything you do with that direction.

This is not about creating pretty posters. It is about getting absolute clarity on where you are going so you can say no to everything that does not serve that future.

broken.label: When this is broken
broken.content: You change direction constantly based on whatever opportunity appears. You cannot describe your business in three years beyond "bigger and more profitable." Every decision feels like starting from scratch because there is no consistent direction to judge against. Your team works hard but without alignment, everyone pulling in slightly different directions. Growth feels random and reactive rather than purposeful.

ok.label: When this is OK
ok.content: You have a rough sense of where you want to go but it is vague and changes when you talk about it. The team knows the general direction but not the specifics. You make decent decisions most of the time but occasionally get distracted by shiny objects that do not fit. Your vision exists in your head but is not written down or shared consistently. Some quarters feel aligned, others feel scattered.

working.label: When this is working
working.content: You can paint a vivid, specific picture of your business in three years that energises you and your team. Every strategic decision gets measured against this vision, does it take you closer or further away? The team understands not just what you do, but where you are going and why. You say no to good opportunities because they do not serve the bigger picture. Growth is deliberate, not random.

thingsToThinkAbout: Can you describe the future clearly and confidently? Not "we want to be successful" but specific outcomes. How many customers? What services? What does a typical day look like? If you cannot describe it specifically, you cannot build toward it deliberately. Vague visions create vague results.

How big do you truly want this to become? There is no wrong answer, lifestyle business, regional player, or global empire are all valid choices. But the choice shapes everything: how you price, who you hire, what systems you build. Be honest about your real ambition, not what you think you should want.

What will you refuse to compromise? Every business faces pressure to compromise on quality, values, or focus. Defining your non-negotiables in advance makes those decisions easier when the pressure is on. What lines will you not cross to grow faster? What standards will you never lower?

Does growth increase or reduce freedom? Growth for growth's sake can trap you in a business you no longer want to run. Be clear about whether the vision you are building will give you more freedom or less. If your vision requires you to work 80-hour weeks forever, is that really what you want?

Does execution build toward the intended future? Look at what you actually did last quarter. Strip away the urgent stuff and the firefighting. Did the remaining work move you toward your vision or just keep the lights on? If most of your time goes to maintenance rather than building, something needs to change.
actionsIntro: When you open this playbook in the Profit System, you will find:
quickWins: This week: Write a one-page description of what your business looks like in three years. Make it specific, who works there, what you sell, what a typical week looks like for you. Do not worry about making it perfect. Just get it out of your head and onto paper.

This week: List three things you are currently working on that do not serve your long-term vision. Pick one to stop or delegate immediately. Vision without subtraction is just addition with better branding.

This week: Share your vision draft with two people whose judgment you trust. Ask them: does this sound like something you would be excited to be part of? Does it sound achievable? Their questions will help you sharpen it.

detailSections.title: What to Do
detailSections.content:
Create a specific, detailed description of what your business and life look like in three years. Not goals, not metrics, not a strategy document. A vivid picture. Something you can see, feel, and describe to someone else as if you were already living it. This becomes the north star for every major decision you make.

Write it in present tense as if it is already happening. "I wake up at..." not "I want to wake up at..."

detailSections.title: Why It Matters
detailSections.content:
Most business owners either have no long-term picture (they just react to what is in front of them) or they have vague aspirations ("I want to grow the business"). Neither is useful for making decisions. When you have a clear, detailed 3-year picture, decisions become simple: "Does this move me toward the picture or away from it?"

Verne Harnish calls this the BHAG. Dan Sullivan calls it the 25-year framework. We are using three years because it is far enough to be ambitious and close enough to feel real. You can actually imagine yourself there. Five or ten years is too abstract for most owners to use as a practical decision-making tool.

detailSections.title: How to Use This
detailSections.content:
Find 90 minutes of uninterrupted time. Not at your desk. Not between meetings. Go somewhere that helps you think clearly. Work through each section below, writing at least a full paragraph for each.

---

detailSections.title: Describe Your Business in Three Years
detailSections.content:
- [ ] **Revenue and profit** - What is the annual revenue? What is the profit margin? Write specific numbers, not ranges. "The business turns over £1.2M with a 25% net margin" is better than "somewhere between £1M and £2M."
- [ ] **Team** - How many people work in the business? What roles exist? Which are employees and which are contractors? What is the culture like? Describe a typical team meeting.
- [ ] **Clients** - Who are you serving? How many clients do you have? What do they pay? What do they say about working with you?
- [ ] **Products and services** - What do you sell? Has the offer evolved? What have you stopped doing? What have you added?
- [ ] ⚡ Quick Win: **Describe a typical Monday morning in your business three years from now** - Where are you? What is the first thing you do? Who do you talk to? This single exercise often reveals more about what you want than any amount of goal-setting.

detailSections.title: Describe Your Life in Three Years
detailSections.content:
- [ ] **Daily routine** - What does a normal day look like from waking up to going to bed? Where do you live? How do you commute (or not)?
- [ ] **Family and relationships** - How much time do you spend with family? What has changed from today? What experiences are you having together?
- [ ] **Health and energy** - How fit are you? What are you doing to maintain it? How much do you sleep?
- [ ] **Financial position** - Beyond business profit, what does your personal financial picture look like? Savings, investments, debts cleared?

detailSections.title: Stress-Test the Picture
detailSections.content:
- [ ] **Does the business picture support the life picture?** - Many owners describe a life of freedom and a business that requires 60-hour weeks. These are incompatible. Adjust one or both until they align.
- [ ] **Is this YOUR picture or someone else's?** - Are you describing what you genuinely want, or what you think you should want? Family expectations, peer pressure, and social media can distort vision. Be honest.
- [ ] **What are you willing to sacrifice to get there?** - Every vision has a cost. More revenue might mean more travel. A bigger team means more management. Name the trade-offs and decide if you accept them.
- [ ] **Read it aloud to someone you trust** - Does it resonate when you say it out loud? Does it excite you or does it sound like a business plan you wrote for a bank? If it does not light you up, keep refining.

---

detailSections.title: Expected Outcome
detailSections.content:
- A written, present-tense description of your business and life in three years
- Specific enough to use as a decision-making filter ("Does this serve the picture?")
- Stress-tested for internal consistency between business ambition and lifestyle desire
- Shared with at least one trusted person for accountability
- A document you revisit quarterly to check alignment

detailSections.title: Coach Notes
detailSections.content:
This is one of the most powerful exercises in the Profit System, but only if the client goes deep enough. Surface-level answers ("I want more revenue and more free time") are useless. Push for specifics. "How much revenue? How much free time? What will you do with it? Where will you be?"

Watch for two failure modes. First, the client who writes what they think you want to hear rather than what they genuinely want. Some owners actually want a small, profitable lifestyle business but feel ashamed to admit it. Validate whatever they truly want. Second, the client whose business picture and life picture are contradictory. This is a coaching goldmine. Helping them resolve that tension often unlocks clarity they have never had before.

detailSections.title: What to Do
detailSections.content:
Define exactly how big you want your business to become and what trade-offs you are willing to make to get there. This means putting specific numbers against revenue, team size, market reach, and complexity. Then honestly assessing whether you are prepared for what those numbers actually require.

Many owners chase growth without ever deciding how much is enough. This action forces that decision.

detailSections.title: Why It Matters
detailSections.content:
Growth for its own sake is a trap. A business doing £500K with two people and 30% margins might be a better life than a business doing £3M with fifteen people and 8% margins. But you cannot make that comparison unless you have thought clearly about what each scenario actually looks like day to day.

Every growth tier brings new complexity. £200K-£500K is mostly about the owner doing the work. £500K-£1M requires building a small team. £1M-£3M demands management systems, middle layers, and real leadership. £3M-£5M is a different game entirely. The skills, stress, and trade-offs are completely different at each level. Choose deliberately.

detailSections.title: How to Use This
detailSections.content:
Work through the sections below with total honesty. There are no wrong answers. A deliberately small business is just as valid as an ambitious growth plan. The only wrong answer is one you have not thought through.

---

detailSections.title: Define Your Target Scale
detailSections.content:
- [ ] **What is your target annual revenue in 3 years?** - Write a specific number. Then write what that number actually requires in terms of clients, products, pricing, and sales volume.
- [ ] **What is your target team size?** - How many people, in what roles? Full-time employees, part-time, contractors? Be specific.
- [ ] **What is your target profit margin?** - Revenue means nothing without margin. A £2M business at 5% profit pays the owner £100K. A £500K business at 30% pays £150K.
- [ ] ⚡ Quick Win: **Calculate your personal income at your target revenue and margin** - Does the number excite you or disappoint you? Adjust until it works.
- [ ] **What market reach do you want?** - Local, regional, national, international? Niche specialist or broad service provider?

detailSections.title: Assess the Trade-offs
detailSections.content:
- [ ] **Hours per week** - At your target scale, how many hours per week will you realistically work? Not the fantasy number. The realistic one based on what that size of business demands.
- [ ] **Management burden** - At your target team size, how much of your time goes to managing people versus doing the work you enjoy? A team of 15 means you are a manager first, practitioner second.
- [ ] **Complexity tolerance** - More revenue usually means more products, more systems, more things that can break. Rate your tolerance for complexity 1-10. Be honest.
- [ ] **Financial risk** - Bigger businesses carry bigger overheads. What fixed costs will you carry at your target scale? Can you sleep at night with that number going out every month?
- [ ] **Lifestyle impact** - What changes at home? More travel? Less flexibility? More financial security but less time? Name the specific impacts.

detailSections.title: Choose Your Path
detailSections.content:
- [ ] **Lifestyle business (optimise for freedom)** - Keep it small, keep it simple, maximise profit per hour. Nothing wrong with this. Many of the happiest business owners chose this path deliberately.
- [ ] **Growth business (optimise for scale)** - Build something bigger than yourself. Accept the management overhead. Invest in systems and people. This path requires a fundamentally different mindset.
- [ ] **Write a one-paragraph "ambition statement"** - "I am building a [size] business that [does what] because [why]. I am willing to [trade-offs] to achieve this."
- [ ] **Check alignment with your 3-Year Picture** - Does your ambition statement match what you described in [[3.1 Vision]]? If not, one of them needs to change.

---

detailSections.title: Expected Outcome
detailSections.content:
- A specific, numbers-based definition of what "success" means for your business
- Clear understanding of the trade-offs required at your chosen scale
- An honest assessment of whether you are prepared for those trade-offs
- An ambition statement that guides strategic decisions
- Alignment between scale ambitions and lifestyle desires

detailSections.title: Coach Notes
detailSections.content:
This action often produces uncomfortable realisations. Some clients discover they have been chasing a target that, once examined, they do not actually want. Others realise they have been playing too small because they never gave themselves permission to think bigger. Both realisations are valuable.

The most common coaching moment is the client who wants £2M revenue and a 4-day week with no direct reports. Those numbers rarely coexist. Help them see the mismatch without being judgmental. The goal is clarity, not a "correct" answer. Some clients will need to revisit this action multiple times as their ambitions evolve. That is normal and healthy.

detailSections.title: What to Do
detailSections.content:
Identify the absolute standards, values, and lifestyle requirements that you will never compromise for the sake of growth. These are your hard lines. The things that, if you cross them, make the business not worth running even if it is profitable.

Write them down, share them with your team, and use them as a filter every time a decision tempts you to bend.

detailSections.title: Why It Matters
detailSections.content:
Growth creates pressure to compromise. A big client wants to push your boundaries. A new revenue stream requires working weekends. A cost-saving measure lowers quality. Without clearly defined non-negotiables, these compromises happen gradually. No single decision feels like a betrayal. But after two years of small compromises, you wake up running a business you do not recognise.

Jim Collins found that enduringly great companies had a set of core values they refused to sacrifice regardless of external pressure. The same principle applies at any scale. Your non-negotiables are the walls of the container. Everything else can flex. These cannot.

detailSections.title: How to Use This
detailSections.content:
Work through each category below. Not every category will have a non-negotiable for you. That is fine. The goal is to identify the handful (usually 5-8) that genuinely matter, not to fill in every box.

---

detailSections.title: Quality Standards
detailSections.content:
- [ ] **Define your quality floor** - What is the minimum standard of work you will deliver, regardless of time pressure or budget? Describe it specifically enough that anyone on your team could recognise when work falls below it.
- [ ] **Identify quality compromises you have already made** - Be honest. Where have you let standards slip? What triggered it? This reveals where you are most vulnerable.
- [ ] ⚡ Quick Win: **Name one quality standard you want to restore right now** - Something that used to be better. Commit to bringing it back this week.

detailSections.title: Ethical Lines
detailSections.content:
- [ ] **Write down practices you refuse to engage in** - Misleading marketing, aggressive upselling, copying competitors, underpaying suppliers, making promises you cannot keep. Be specific.
- [ ] **Define how you treat people who cannot fight back** - Suppliers, junior staff, unsuccessful applicants, departing employees. Your ethics are tested when there are no consequences for bad behaviour.
- [ ] **Identify any current grey areas** - Anything you are doing that you feel slightly uncomfortable about. Bring it into the light and decide: is this acceptable or does it need to change?

detailSections.title: Lifestyle Requirements
detailSections.content:
- [ ] **Set your working hours boundary** - Maximum hours per week. Days you do not work. Times you are unavailable. Write them as rules, not guidelines.
- [ ] **Define your family and personal commitments** - School pick-ups, date nights, holidays, exercise, hobbies. What is protected time that business cannot touch?
- [ ] **Set your minimum personal income** - Below this number, the business is not serving you. You are serving it. What is the floor?
- [ ] ⚡ Quick Win: **Block one personal commitment in your calendar this week and treat it as unmovable** - Show yourself (and your team) that non-negotiables are real.

detailSections.title: Relationship Standards
detailSections.content:
- [ ] **Define the client you will not work with** - What behaviour, attitude, or situation makes someone a bad fit regardless of how much they pay? Describe the red flags clearly.
- [ ] **Set your team culture standards** - What behaviour is unacceptable from team members? Dishonesty, disrespect, blaming, gossip? Name it and enforce it.
- [ ] **Write your "walk away" criteria** - Under what circumstances would you fire a profitable client, decline a lucrative deal, or let a high-performer go? Pre-deciding makes it easier when the moment arrives.

detailSections.title: Compile and Share
detailSections.content:
- [ ] **Write your non-negotiables on one page** - Maximum 8 items. Each stated clearly in one sentence. This becomes your reference document.
- [ ] **Share them with your team** - Non-negotiables only work if everyone knows them. Discuss them in a team meeting. Invite questions.
- [ ] **Review them quarterly** - Not to weaken them. To check whether you are living them. Are there any you have already compromised? Fix it.

---

detailSections.title: Expected Outcome
detailSections.content:
- A clear, written list of 5-8 non-negotiable standards and values
- Specific enough to be actionable, not vague platitudes
- Shared with your team so they can uphold them too
- A decision-making filter that prevents slow-creep compromise
- Quarterly review habit to maintain accountability

detailSections.title: Coach Notes
detailSections.content:
The power of this action is in specificity. "We deliver great service" is a platitude, not a non-negotiable. "Every client gets a response within 4 hours during working days" is a non-negotiable. Push clients past vague statements into concrete, testable standards.

The lifestyle non-negotiables are often the hardest for driven owners to commit to. They feel guilty about setting boundaries. Remind them that a burned-out owner is useless to their business, their team, and their family. Boundaries are not weakness. They are what make sustained high performance possible. If a client has already compromised a non-negotiable and feels bad about it, that is a coaching opportunity. Help them restore it and build the discipline to protect it going forward.

detailSections.title: What to Do
detailSections.content:
Review every current strategy, project, and priority against your 3-year vision. Score each one for alignment. Kill anything that does not serve the bigger picture. Start anything that is missing. This is a ruthless pruning exercise that clears the path between where you are now and where you said you want to be.

Do this after completing The 3-Year Picture and Scale & Ambition Clarity. Without a clear vision, there is nothing to audit against.

detailSections.title: Why It Matters
detailSections.content:
Most businesses accumulate projects, priorities, and commitments the way a house accumulates clutter. Each one seemed like a good idea at the time. But collectively, they pull the business in ten directions instead of one. Strategy is as much about what you stop doing as what you start doing.

Michael Porter said it clearly: "The essence of strategy is choosing what not to do." This audit forces that choice. Every project that does not serve your vision is consuming resources, time, energy, and attention that could go toward something that does.

detailSections.title: How to Use This
detailSections.content:
Gather your list of current projects, commitments, and priorities before starting. If you do not have a written list, that is your first task. Then work through the scoring and decision framework below.

---

detailSections.title: List Everything on Your Plate
detailSections.content:
- [ ] **Write down every active project in the business** - Product development, marketing campaigns, system implementations, hiring plans, partnerships. Everything that is consuming time and resources right now.
- [ ] **List every recurring commitment** - Regular meetings, reports, memberships, subscriptions, retainers, partnerships. Anything that takes time or money on a repeating basis.
- [ ] **Include "someday" projects** - Things you have been planning or talking about but have not started. They are taking up mental space even if they are not taking up calendar space.
- [ ] ⚡ Quick Win: **Count the total number of items on your list** - If it is more than 15, you are almost certainly spread too thin. The number itself is diagnostic.

detailSections.title: Score Each Item for Vision Alignment
detailSections.content:
- [ ] **Rate each item 1-5 for alignment with your 3-year picture** - 5 = directly serves the vision. 3 = somewhat related. 1 = no connection at all.
- [ ] **Rate each item 1-5 for current ROI (time and money invested vs. results)** - 5 = excellent return. 1 = consuming resources with little to show for it.
- [ ] **Multiply alignment x ROI for a composite score out of 25** - This gives you a quick ranking. Items scoring below 10 are prime candidates for cutting.
- [ ] **Flag anything scoring 5 or below** - These are actively working against your vision. They need to stop immediately unless there is a contractual or legal reason they cannot.

detailSections.title: Make the Hard Decisions
detailSections.content:
- [ ] **Kill list: Write down everything you will stop doing** - Projects to cancel, meetings to end, commitments to wind down. Be specific about when each one stops.
- [ ] **Keep list: Confirm what stays and why** - For every item you keep, write one sentence explaining how it serves the vision. If you cannot, it belongs on the kill list.
- [ ] **Start list: What is missing?** - Look at your 3-year picture. What do you need to start doing that you are not currently doing? This is where the freed-up resources go.
- [ ] **Communicate changes to your team** - If you are killing projects, the people working on them need to know. Explain the reasoning. Connect it to the vision.
- [ ] ⚡ Quick Win: **Cancel or decline one low-scoring commitment today** - A meeting, a project, a subscription. Start the pruning immediately.

detailSections.title: Build an Alignment Check Habit
detailSections.content:
- [ ] **Before saying yes to anything new, run the alignment test** - "Does this serve my 3-year picture? What is the expected ROI? What will I stop doing to make room for it?"
- [ ] **Schedule this full audit quarterly** - Projects drift. New commitments creep in. A quarterly audit keeps the business focused on what matters.
- [ ] **Share your framework with key team members** - When everyone understands the vision alignment filter, they start filtering their own ideas and requests. This is how strategy cascades through an organisation.

---

detailSections.title: Expected Outcome
detailSections.content:
- A scored list of every current project and commitment ranked by vision alignment
- A clear kill list of projects and commitments to end
- A start list of missing initiatives to begin
- A simple alignment test to apply to every future decision
- A quarterly audit habit that prevents strategic drift

detailSections.title: Coach Notes
detailSections.content:
This is often an emotional exercise. Clients have invested time, money, and identity into projects that score poorly. Killing a project can feel like admitting failure. Reframe it: stopping something that does not serve your vision is not failure. Continuing it is. The sunk cost fallacy is powerful here.

The most common resistance is "but we have already invested so much in X." Remind them that past investment is irrelevant. The only question is: does continuing this serve where you are going? If not, every additional hour and pound spent on it is waste. Watch for clients who score everything highly to avoid making hard choices. If every project scores 20+, they are not being honest. Push back and force genuine prioritisation.
